cot_prefix: Okay, let's look at this question. It's about the passage discussing the effects of faking smiles versus genuine ones. The question is asking what is implied about deep acting. First, I need to recall what deep acting is. According to the passage, deep acting is thinking of pleasant memories to make oneself smile. The study compared surface acting (fake smiles) and deep acting. The key part here is the last paragraph where Dr. Scott says that although deep acting can improve moods a little in the short term, it's not a long-term solution. He also mentions that if you do this over a long period, you might start to feel unreal and not feel like yourself anymore. Now looking at the choices: (A) doesn't have any effect on men - The passage does mention that women were helped more by deep acting than men, but it doesn't say deep acting has no effect on men. It just says women benefited more, but doesn't say men aren't affected at all. (B) cannot improve our moods in any case - The passage says deep acting can improve moods a little in the short term, so this is incorrect. (C) harms our feelings in the long run - The passage says if done over a long period, it can cause you to feel unreal and not like yourself. This might be considered as harming feelings in the
